    • Lab#3 Building a Rest API to support the creation of a new account and customer details

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
• mkdocs new [dir-name] - Create a new project.
  • mkdocs serve - Start the live-reloading docs server.
  • mkdocs build - Build the documentation site.
  • mkdocs -h - Print help message and exit.

docs.title: Project layout
docs.text:
mkdocs.yml    # The configuration file.
docs/
    index.md  # The documentation homepage.
    ...       # Other markdown pages, images and other files.

docs.title: API Endpoints
docs.text:
GET /sayHello

Test GET API request with Account Controller

POST /account

Create a new customer

Request: 

{
    "name": "Joe Bloggs",
    "email": "joe@gmail.com",
    "mobileNumber": "086-123456"
}
Response 201 (Account Created): 
{
    "statusCode": "201",
    "statusMsg": "Account created successfully"
}
Response 400 (Customer Exists): 
{
    "apiPath": "uri=/api/account",
    "errorCode": "400 BAD_REQUEST",
    "errorMessage": "Customer already registerd with given mobile Number 086-123456",
    "errortime": "2026-04-02T19:02:29.2406524"
}

GET /account?mobileNumber

Fetch the customer details by phone number

Response 200 OK: 

{
    "accountsDto": {
        "accountNumber": 1624831019,
        "accountType": "Savings",
        "branchAddress": "123 Main Street, New York"
    },
    "email": "joe@gmail.com",
    "mobileNumber": "086-123456",
    "name": "Joe Bloggs"
}

Response 404 Not Found: 

{
    "apiPath": "uri=/api/account",
    "errorCode": "404 NOT_FOUND",
    "errorMessage": "Customer not found with the given input data mobileNumber : '086-123457'",
    "errortime": "2026-04-02T19:51:51.4683013"
}

docs.title: Labs
docs.text:
1. Getting started with Springboot – Helloworld Example
  2. Configuring H2 DB and YAML application.properties
  3. Building a Rest API to support the creation of a new account and customer details
  4. Exception Handling –check if customer already exists
  5. Implementing a READ API that fetches the details based on mobile number

docs.title: Lab#1 Getting started with Springboot – Helloworld Example
docs.text:
1. Create a new project from Spring.io. Add the dependencies shown. Java 17 is required for Springboot 3.

  1. Generate and Import the Maven Project into your IDE.

  2. Now Add a Restcontroller class to your project as shown below.

  1. Test the application.

docs.title: Lab#2 Configuring H2 DB and YAML application.properties
docs.text:
In this lab we will continue from the previous lab and configure the application to use an in-memory H2 database.

  1. Rename application.properties as application.yml and update with following properties (file provided)

  1. Create a file called scheme.sql (provided) in the resources folder with the following data.

  1. Restart the server. It will start on port 8080 based on yml file.
  2. Go to the h2-console in the browser. You should see the two tables have bee created.

Now we will write Spring Data JPA entities & repositories to interact with DB tables

  1. Create a new package for the entity classes as show below.

  1. Add the classes BaseEntity, Accounts and Customers (given) and examine the code.

  1. Now Add the Repository interfaces

We will use the Data Transfer Object pattern to transfer data – not the entity classes themselves.

  1. Create a new package with class AccountsDto and CustomerDto. These classes uses Lombok (You may need to turn on annotations in your IDE or install a Lombok jar)

  1. Also add a ResponseDto and an ErrorResponseDto class

docs.title: Lab#3 Building a Rest API to support the creation of a new account and customer details.
docs.text:
In this lab we are creating the API that will allow the creation of a new account and customer.

Note: If you have problems with Lombok https://stackoverflow.com/questions/35842751/lombok-not-working-with-sts

  1. Add class AccountsConstants provided to a new package com.tus.accounts.constants. This class will store error messages.

  1. Update the AccountController class for the PostMapping as shown. You can add /accounts to the PostMapping path

  1. Add a packages service and serviceImpl. These will hold the interface for the Service layer and its implementation. Note: don’t need to use @Autowired in Springboot 3.

  1. We will now create a mapper class (code given) for AccountsMapper and CustomerMapper. This is to map data between the Entity classes and the DTO classes.

  1. Update the AccountsServiceImpl with the method for creating a new account base on a CustomerDto object.

  1. Update the controller to call the service layer

  1. Run the project and check the h2-console. The tables should be created and empty.

  1. Create a new customer from postman. You should get response code 201 and message as shown below.

  1. Check that the data has been written to the database

docs.title: Lab#4 Exception Handling –check if customer already exists
docs.text:
The customers mobile phone number must be unique. In this lab we will check if a customer with the given phone number already exists and we will reject the request accordingly.

  1. Add a package com.tus.accounts.exception with a class CustomerAlreadyExistsException

  1. In the CustomerRepository interface add a query to find a Customer based on the mobile number. (This may be in your file already).

  1. Now in the Service class add code that will throw the CustomerAlreadyExistsException.

  1. Add a class in the exception package called GlobalExceptionHandler. This will handle exceptions in one location rather than duplicating the handlng. It is annotated with @ControllerAdvice and will handle the exception when thrown by the controller.

  1. Test the application. Add a customer. Customer is created successfully. Then add a customer with the same mobile number again. The request is rejected with error message as shown.

docs.title: Lab#5 Implementing a READ API that fetches the details based on mobile number.
docs.text:
In this lab we are going to fetch the customer and account details based on the customers mobile number. We will find the customer object base on the mobile number. Then we will find the account based on the customer id. The account data is returned as part of the CustomerDto object. The case where a customer does not exist is handled with exceptions.

  1. First we need to add a check to see if customer already exists. Add the method findByCustomerId in AccountsRepository interface.

  1. Add a new ResourceNotFoundException the com.tus.accounts.exception package.

  1. Update GlobalLogicExceptionHandler to handle the exception and return an appropriate ErrorResponseDto.

  1. Update the CustomerDto to add a new field to keep the account information. We could create a new Dto for the combined Customer and Account information but will leave like this for now.

  1. Add a new method to the controller class for the read API.

  1. Implement the fetchAccount method by adding to the Service Interface and the implementation class. This uses Lambda expressions to throw the exception.

  1. Test the Application. Add a customer and then fetch the details as shown. Then use a different phone number and check that the 404 response with appropriate error message is found.
